title: Emergency Water Storage
summary: Store at least one gallon of safe water per person per day for a minimum of three days in clean food-grade containers. Plan for home, vehicle, and workplace storage, and rotate supplies regularly.
quickAnswer: Store at least one gallon of water per person per day for three days minimum, in food-grade containers kept in a cool dark place, and rotate every six months.
whenToUse: Use before emergencies to build a reliable water reserve at home, in your vehicle, or at work.
Use when you still have safe water and time to prepare for storms, outages, or disaster disruption.
preferredAction: Store safe tap or bottled water in clean, tightly sealed food-grade containers in cool, dark locations away from chemicals.
backupAction: If ideal containers are unavailable, carefully reuse suitable beverage bottles such as PET soda bottles after proper cleaning and sanitizing.
stepByStepActions: Calculate your water needs: at least one gallon per person per day, with extra for hot climates, children, pets, illness, or special medical needs.
Choose food-grade containers that close tightly and have never stored chemicals, gasoline, or pesticides.
Wash, rinse, sanitize, and dry self-filled containers before use.
Fill containers with safe tap or bottled water and seal tightly.
Label each container with the fill date and mark as drinking water.
Store containers in a cool, dark, protected location away from sunlight, heat, fuels, pesticides, and chemical vapors.
Rotate self-filled stored water every six months by emptying, cleaning, and refilling.
warnings: Use only food-grade containers; do not reuse containers that previously held toxic substances, and note that vapors from gasoline, pesticides, and chemicals can penetrate some plastics.
Milk jugs and some food containers are poor long-term choices because residues can promote bacterial growth.
Stored water needs to be rotated and refreshed periodically to remain safe.
whatNotToDo: Do not keep topping off old water without emptying, cleaning, and refilling.
Do not assume unknown plastics are food-grade.
redFlags: Stored water smells foul, appears slimy, or has visible growth.
An emergency occurs and you have no safe stored water and the public supply is unsafe.
Water was stored in containers that previously held toxic substances.
preparednessTips: Plan extra water for hot climates, children, medical needs, and pets.
Aim for a two-week supply if space and budget allow.
Store smaller grab-and-go bottles as well as larger home containers.
Use reminders to rotate stored water every six months.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency Disinfection of Drinking Water
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Step-by-step emergency boiling and bleach methods, plus container guidance.

sourceReferences.title: How to Make Water Safe in an Emergency
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Confirms primary treatment methods and explains treatment limits.

sourceReferences.title: How to Create and Store an Emergency Water Supply
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary household storage quantities, container handling, cleaning, and rotation guidance.

notes: Consolidated from emergency-water-storage (canonical) and emergency-water-storage-at-home (archived). Steps merged: 7 total (6 from archive guide's more detailed sequence, plus canonical's broader scope elements). Layer upgraded from scenario to preparedness to match content nature. Guide type upgraded to preparedness_guide. Source refs from canonical (3 real URLs); archive's refs pointed to the same CDC page. Archive slug should be set to review_status:archived after this version is approved.
constraintAnnotationNotes: This is a preparedness guide about storing water in advance. It does not depend on specific equipment or conditions that would be blocked by any canonical constraints — you can store water regardless of whether you can boil, have bleach, etc. It does not become more relevant under any specific constraint either, since storage is a universal preparedness action. Role is 'supporting' because the 72-Hour Emergency Supplies parentTopic has no primary preparedness guide currently, but this guide covers a narrow subtopic (water only) rather than the full kit, so 'supporting' is appropriate. No alternative slugs are needed as this is a standalone preparedness guide.

tools.name: Container

tools.context: Food-grade containers are essential for safely storing emergency drinking water.

tools.name: Bleach

tools.context: Used to sanitize self-filled containers before storing water.
